fr:mainmatter.html:p: One idea that we'll use a lot this semester is the idea of a set. This is an idea that nowadays is studied mainly in mathematics, but its history is closely intertwined with the history of logic itself. Logic and set theory grew up together, with their childhood in the late 19th/early 20th century, their awkward teen years through the mid to late 20th century, and they're really coming into their young adulthood well these days. Because they're so closely related, you can't really study either of them without learning at least a little about the other.

For this semester, we will need only some very basic set theory, but we do need it. In particular, the idea of a set is central to the ideas of an argument and a logic, and we need those ideas on the table to really get rolling this semester. So this week's first goal is to introduce you to these three ideas---first sets, then arguments, and then logics. The second goal is to look at some different questions we can ask about logics: what they're like and how they relate to each other.

A set is a collection of things, any things at all, thought about as if the collection were its own separate thing. For example, there is a set of the students enrolled in ATS2866, and there is a set of all the Facebook accounts, and there is a set of all the even natural numbers. (The "natural numbers" are 0, 1, 2, \ldots .)

Let's take the set of the students enrolled in ATS2866. You are (probably) a member of this set. (I mean, you're more than welcome to be reading this even if you're not! It's just that I suspect that most readers are.) The set itself is not a student; it is something else: a set. If you smell metaphysics lurking around the corner here, you're not mistaken. But let's not turn that corner; let's just see how we work with sets.

In working with sets, we need to know how to indicate which set we're talking about. There are two standard ways we can use to talk about particular sets: by listing the members, or by giving a description of something that the members have in common, and that nothing else has.

To give a set by listing the members, we give the list between curly braces. So, for example, \{p, q, r\} is a set that contains the three atomic formulas p, q, r, and doesn't contain anything else. And \{Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Adelaide, Perth, Hobart, Darwin, Canberra\} is a set that contains those eight cities and doesn't contain anything else.

To give a set by description, we just say what's in it. So when I say "the set of students enrolled in ATS2866", I've specified a set in this way. I didn't list all the students; I just gave a description that applies to all and only the students enrolled in ATS2866. This is the other main way of specifying a set. As another example, we can think of the set of all the Australian state and internal territory capitals. This set contains the same eight cities as the set listed above. (The Jervis Bay Territory is another internal territory, but it has no capital.)

All that matters to a set is what's in it, what its members are. There isn't anything else to the identity of a set than the identities of its members. This property of sets is called extensionality. This means that the set \{Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Adelaide, Perth, Hobart, Darwin, Canberra\} is the very same set as the set of Australian state and internal territory capitals. Same members, same set.

This also means that there's a potential for misunderstanding that we need to head off: although we can (and we very much will) use lists to specify some sets, sets are not lists. The reason is that there is more to lists than just what things they contain: lists also have order, and can contain the same thing multiple times. Sets, though, do not have order, and they do not contain things any particular number of times---they either contain them or they do not, full stop.

So think about the list "Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Adelaide, Perth, Hobart, Darwin, Canberra". This is not the same list as "Canberra, Melbourne, Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Darwin, Hobart, Perth, Melbourne, Adelaide". The two lists differ in order, and the first one contains Melbourne just once, while the second contains it three times.

But although these are not the same list, the set \{Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Adelaide, Perth, Hobart, Darwin, Canberra\} is the very same set as the set \{Canberra, Melbourne, Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Darwin, Hobart, Perth, Melbourne, Adelaide\}. That's because these sets contain the very same things: just these eight cities, and nothing else. Even though I've used two different lists to talk about this set, the set I'm talking about remains the same. This is key to thinking about sets: all that matters is which things are members. It doesn't matter what we call those things, or what order we list them in, or whether we list things more than once.

There's another upshot of this: a set can't change which things are its members. Any supposed change in membership just means we're talking about a different set. Like, consider the set of students enrolled in ATS2866. That's a set containing certain people and not others. If your friend enrols in ATS2866 tomorrow, it's not that that very set would come to contain a new member; that's not possible. It's rather that the description "the set of students enrolled in ATS2866" would come to indicate a different set than it currently does: one that includes your friend as well.

There is a set with no members, called the empty set. We can talk about the empty set using our list-the-members notation by writing \{\}---the curly braces with nothing listed inside them---or just by saying "the empty set". Note that, by extensionality, there can only be one empty set.
The most important kind of relationship between sets is the subset relation.
A set \Gamma is a subset of a set \Delta iff: every member of \Gamma is also a member of \Delta . There's a symbol for this: \Gamma \subseteq \Delta means that \Gamma is a subset of \Delta .

In the above definition, \Gamma and \Delta are capital Greek letters: \Gamma is Gamma and \Delta is Delta. It's common to use lots of different alphabets in logic, with the Greek alphabet probably the most common one other than our own Latin alphabet. I'll use capital Greek letters like these particularly for sets of things, usually sets of formulas.

When \Gamma \subseteq \Delta , we know that everything in \Gamma is in \Delta as well. There might be some other things also in \Delta as well, or there might not; that doesn't matter. (If there isn't anything else in \Delta , then by extensionality \Gamma and \Delta must be the same set.)

Some things to note about subsets:
There is an important difference between being a subset of a set and being a member of the set. For example, \{p, q\} is a subset of \{p, q, r\}, since every member of the former is also a member of the latter. But \{p, q\} is not a member of \{p, q, r\}; only p, q, and r are members of this set.
Every set is a subset of itself. For example, consider the set \{p, q \land r\}. This has two members: p and q \land r. And both of these members are indeed also members of \{p, q \land r\}, so the definition of subsethood is met. More generally, for any set \Gamma , all the members of \Gamma are indeed members of \Gamma , so \Gamma is always a subset of itself, for any \Gamma .
The empty set is a subset of absolutely any set. This might be a surprise, but remember logicians' weird habit number 2! To check whether the empty set is a subset of some set \Gamma , we need to check whether all the members of the empty set are also members of \Gamma . Since there are no members of the empty set, indeed it is true that all the members of the empty set are members of \Gamma ---no matter what \Gamma is. (Using logicians' weird habit number 2, it's even true that all the members of the empty set are square circles, that they're all plants with mammal-like brains, and that they're all behind you right now.)

There are a couple of operations on sets that are useful to know about, called intersection and union. These are not super-central to this unit, but they will come up now and again. (Intersection will turn out to be more useful for our purposes than union.)

Given sets \Gamma and \Delta , their intersection \Gamma \cap \Delta is the set whose members are exactly the things that are members of both \Gamma and \Delta . Their union \Gamma \cup \Delta is the set whose members are exactly the things that are members of either \Gamma or \Delta . (Remember logicians' weird habit 1 here!)
For example, the intersection of \{p, q, r\} and \{q, r, s\} is \{q, r\}; and the union of these same two sets is \{p, q, r, s\}.

Intersections and unions don't need to be between exactly two sets: you can take the intersection of a whole bunch of sets, and it contains whatever they all have in common; and you can likewise take the union of a whole bunch of sets, and it contains whatever is contained in any of them.

That barely scratches the surface of what can be said about sets, but it's enough for us to go on. One way sets are involved in what we'll do is through the idea of an argument, which will absolutely central for this semester. Just like our logical "languages" have something in common with real languages, but are highly idealized, our logical "arguments" too will have a very simple and very precise shape, unlike real arguments.

The kind of argument you should be thinking of here is not a debate or exchange between two parties. It's rather a collection of premises offered in support of a conclusion. (This is the most usual sense of the word "argument" in philosophy.) For our purposes, an argument is always taken from some particular language, and has two parts: a set of premises, which are all sentences of the language, and a single conclusion, which is a sentence of the language.
An argument is a set \Gamma of sentences, called the premises of the argument, together with a sentence A, called the conclusion of the argument. It is written \Gamma \;/\; A.

In writing out particular arguments, I'll often abbreviate by omitting the set braces \{ and \}. So, for example, in the argument p \land q, 
eg r \;/\; 
eg p \lor r, there are two premises: p \land q and 
eg r; and the conclusion is 
eg p \lor r. The set of premises in this argument is \{p \land q, 
eg r\}.

fr:title.text: Do we have to use sets for premises?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Do we have to use sets for premises?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: There certainly are logics where premises have more texture than this! It's not like a law of nature or anything that we use sets here; we can do logic using lists instead, or things called multisets, or really any way you can think of to collect sentences into some larger whole; or we can abandon the idea of collecting up sentences and just look at a single sentence here; or we can do much more besides. But sets give us plenty to work with already for this semester, so we'll stick with them.

An argument always has one conclusion, but it can have any number of premises. That includes some extreme cases: an argument can have zero premises, when it has the empty set of premises; and it can have infinitely many premises. Even when an argument has zero premises, it's important to remember that the argument is still not the same thing as its conclusion. The conclusion is a sentence; the argument is an argument; and these are different kinds of things. For example, the argument \;/\; p \lor \neg p has no premises, and has the sentence p \lor \neg p as its conclusion; but the argument and the sentence remain distinct.

You might worry about how we're going to handle arguments with infinitely many premises. After all, I certainly can't list out infinitely many premises, so I won't be able to write out any such argument in full. But again, this isn't really a problem with infinity: just a big finite argument would already be too much to write out in full. We'll deal with this in the same old way we've dealt with other infinities: by not worrying about it. There are arguments with infinitely many premises; they're out there, they're harmless, and they mostly won't occupy us.

Given a language, we can build up lots of different arguments. Sticking for the moment with an interpretation where an argument is meant to contain premises that support its conclusion, you might notice that different arguments seem to do this more and less well.

For one example, consider the argument p \land q \;/\; p. Using your intuitions about "and" to interpret \land , you can see that this argument's premise seems to support its conclusion. If we suppose that the moon is made of green cheese and Carl is a good dog, we can conclude from that supposition that the moon is made of green cheese. Of course, the moon isn't really made of green cheese, but that's just a reason to question the premise of this argument. As long as we take the premise for granted, it really does support the conclusion.

For a different kind of example, consider the argument p \land q \;/\; r. Here, the conclusion doesn't seem to have anything to do with the premise. If we suppose that the moon is made of green cheese and Carl is a good dog, that doesn't give us any support at all for the claim that Melbourne is in Victoria. Of course, Melbourne really is in Victoria, but that's beside the point; what matters is that this isn't supported by the premise given. Even if we take the premise for granted, the conclusion just doesn't follow.

We're going to start drawing divisions between arguments, between those like p \land q \;/\; p, where the premises do seem to suffice for the conclusion, and those like p \land q \;/\; r, where they do not. But again, we're going to do this in an abstract, and so open-minded, way. Rather than fixing once and for all on a single division between good arguments and bad arguments, we're going to consider all such divisions. Each one is called a logic.
A logic is a set of arguments.

That is, a logic is a set of arguments to be counted as the good ones; any other arguments, outside the set, are to be counted as the bad ones. So any way of choosing a set of arguments gives you a logic. Since there are lots of different sets of arguments you could choose, there are lots of different logics.

How many? This gets further into some differences between different sizes of infinity that we've brushed up against before. The smallest infinity has a few names; it's called both \aleph _{0} and \beth _{0}, and it's the size of the natural numbers 0, 1, 2, \ldots . (\aleph and \beth are the letters aleph and beth, the first two letters of the Hebrew alphabet; the word `alphabet' comes from the names of these two letters. I told you logicians like our alphabets!)

Since we're using a language with infinitely many atomic formulas, if we're going to be precise we need to choose how many that is; let's say we use \beth _{0}, the smallest infinity, as the number of atomic formulas we start from. Then, even after we add all the compound formulas we can form with our connectives, we have \beth _{0} many formulas in total---we've added a lot more formulas (indeed, \beth _{0} many), but the total number has not gone up: \beth _{0} + \beth _{0} = \beth _{0}.

How many arguments can we form with \beth _{0} formulas? Well, each argument starts from a set of premises, and has a conclusion; it turns out that this means that there are \beth _{1} many different arguments. This is a new size of infinity, staggeringly large---it is the same as the number of real numbers (every number on the number line, including like - \pi and \sqrt {200}).

And then, given \beth _{1} arguments, how many different logics are there? The answer turns out to be \beth _{2}, and I have to tell you that my ability to even slightly comprehend the size of this infinity is now completely drowned out. This is beyond beyond unfathomably large. (And, as the notation might suggest, this is really just getting started. How about \beth _{23}?)

This is the kind of thing that set theorists do end up working with, but we won't need it here; I just put it in in case you're interested. If you do want to learn more, you might start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aleph_number or https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beth_number.
The arguments contained in a logic, the ones counted as "good" according to that logic, are called valid in that logic, and the others are called invalid.
An argument is valid in a logic iff it is a member of the logic; otherwise it is invalid in that logic.

Note that it doesn't make sense, on this definition, to talk about an argument's being just plain valid or just plain invalid: it's only with some logic in mind that notions of validity or invalidity make sense. Saying that an argument is valid is like saying that someone is ready. Ready for what? Usually, people are ready for some things and not for others. Similarly, any argument is valid in some logics and not in others. It's only with some particular logic in view that calling an argument valid or invalid is meaningful.

There's not much to say in full generality about validity and invalidity; they really ar just tags for "in a logic" and "out of a logic". We'll look at particular logics with particular applications, though, and then we'll be able to see what validity in these particular cases amounts to; this will vary from logic to logic and application to application.

In full generality, though, I can at least warn you off a potential confusion. As we'll use the terms, it is only arguments that can ever be valid or invalid. It doesn't make any sense, for example, to call a sentence valid.

If you read around, you might find some older sources that will sometimes call a sentence valid. They usually mean by that what we would mean by considering the argument with no premises and that sentence as its conclusion, and calling that argument valid. Here, we'll just avoid ever calling sentences valid or invalid, which makes things simpler.

So if you ever find yourself saying something like "I think this sentence is invalid", you know something's gone wrong. Only arguments can ever be valid or invalid, nothing else; and in its purest sense, this is just a different way of saying that the argument is in a particular logic or not in it.

Since a logic is a set of arguments, you can use everything you know about sets to understand logics. For example, any argument is either in a particular logic or not; it's not in it in any particular order or any particular number of times. There can't be two different logics that have exactly the same valid arguments as each other; that would be just one logic, by extensionality.

There is an empty logic: the empty set. According to the empty logic, no argument is valid, since no argument is in the empty set. And, once we fix a particular language (say our usual one), there is a universal logic, the set of all arguments in that language. According to the universal logic, every argument is valid, since every argument is in that set.

Since logics are sets, they can bear the subset relation to each other as well. For example, the empty logic is a subset of every logic (since the empty set is a subset of every set). As another example, every logic is a subset of the universal logic: any set of arguments must be a subset of the set of all arguments.
And logics have intersections and unions, again since they are sets.

In the weeks to come, we'll meet a number of particular logics and explore a lot of their individual quirks, commonalities, and applications. For now, though, I'll just give you a few more example logics, to help the idea come into focus.
There's some vocabulary that often gets used to talk about relationships between logics: we can say that some logic is stronger or weaker than another.
To say that a logic X is stronger than a logic Y means exactly the same as to say that Y is weaker than X, and they both mean exactly this: that Y is a subset of X.

This isn't a new idea; it's just a couple of new words that we use for the idea of one logic's being a subset of another. So, for example the empty logic is weaker than every logic, and the universal logic is stronger than every logic; that's just a way of saying what you already know about these logics and the subset relation.

Note that there's a potentially confusing feature here: saying that logic X is stronger than logic Y does not mean that X and Y are different logics! Indeed, every logic is both stronger and weaker than itself, since every set is a subset of itself. Again, there's nothing conceptually new here, but the phrasing can be confusing at first; make sure to be careful!

Note, too, that the only way for a logic X to be both stronger and weaker than a logic Y is for X and Y to be the very same logic (by extensionality). But it is common, given two different logics, for neither of them to be stronger or weaker than the other. All it takes is for each of them to contain some argument that the other does not.
Now, return to the five example logics above. Which of these, if any, are stronger or weaker than which of these? Take a moment to think this through before you go on.

html:ul.html:li: Logic 1 is weaker than logic 2, which is to say that 2 is stronger than 1.
Logic 1 is weaker than logic 4, which is to say that 4 is stronger than 1.
Each of logics 1--5, like any logic, is both stronger and weaker than itself.
There are no other relations of strength among these examples.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A whole lot of new ideas this week! The ideas from these first three weeks will be with us all semester long; these are the basic ideas we'll use to frame our study of logic. From this week, you should know what a set is, and how to specify a set, either by listing its members or by describing them. You should know what it means for a set \Gamma to be a subset of a set \Delta , and you should know we're talking about sets when letters like \Gamma and \Delta show up.

You should know what an argument is: a set of formulas (premises) plus a formula (conclusion). And you should know what a logic is: a set of arguments. Finally, you should know what it means for logics to be stronger or weaker.

html:ul.html:li:
Section A: Preliminaries
Lecture 1: Introduction and languages
Lecture 2: Functions and substitutions
Lecture 3: Sets, arguments, and logics

Section B: Structures and matrices
Lecture 4: Structures
[Lecture 5: Matrices and counterexamples]
[Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics]
[Lecture 7: The limits of matrices]

Section C: Exploring intuitionistic logic
Lecture 8: Natural deduction
Lecture 9: Intuitionistic logic
Lecture 10: Intuitionistic models
Lecture 11: Harmony
